Childhood. Why does anyone ask about childhood? It's like people think it's a treasure chest full of life lessons instead of just, you know, years when you were small and stupid. But maybe that's the point, huh? What did I learn? Learning implies I paid attention. That's not my thing.

But okay, what's something that sticks? Mom always said "Laugh loud, Jamie." Not just because she was funny, but because she wanted me to take up space. "They'll try to make you small," she'd say. Funny how that stuck more than the other stuff. The not-being-small part. The loud part.

I don't know if that's the answer they're looking for. Maybe it's too simple. Or maybe it's too much. People like to hear about struggles, about how you overcame. But my childhood wasn't a struggle. It was just life. Mom, school, jokes, more jokes. The regular kind of life.

The regular kind of life that ended up on stage at Comedy Cellar. Ten years old, making a teacher laugh so hard she had to sit down. Maybe that's it. Mom didn't teach me to be a comedian, but she taught me to make people laugh. Taught me that was worth something.

But is that what they're asking? Seems like they want something deeper. Something I maybe didn't realize I learned until now. Ugh, why do I do this to myself? Just say something. Anything. "I learned to make people laugh." There. That's a complete sentence. That's an answer.

But what if they ask why? Why did I learn that? Do I know? Mom used to say it was her way of getting through. The laughter, I mean. After Dad left, after Grandma died, the laughter was what kept us going. That's deep. That's something. Maybe that's the answer.

But maybe it's too much. Maybe it's too soon. I barely know them. Why am I even thinking about this? Just say something light. Keep it simple. "I learned to make people laugh." That's enough. That's good. That's safe.

But isn't that boring? They asked for a lesson. The lesson is that laughter saves you. That's not boring. That's profound. At least, I think it is. Maybe I'm overthinking it.

Just say it. Just say it. "I learned that laughter saves you." There. Done. Now I can move on. Now we can talk about something else. Something easier. Something less... heavy.
response_text: I learned that laughter saves you. Sounds like a punchline, but it's the truth. My mom taught me that, whether she meant to or not. When life gets heavy, make someone laugh. Including yourself.

The thing is, I don't want to sound like I'm complaining. She gave me everything. But I wonder sometimes if I'm still trying to get her approval, even now. Like I'm that eleven-year-old again, just waiting for her to laugh at my jokes.

And then there's the whole other half of the equation that I don't even know. That's the tricky part. How do you shape yourself around something that's just a hole? Maybe that's where the comedy comes in. Filling the silence with something loud.

response_text:
You know, I think about that a lot. My mom was my whole world growing up, and I think I got a lot of my love of performing from her. She was this amazing teacher and choir director, always making people laugh and feel seen. But I also think I inherited some of her anxiety, the need to prove myself.

My relationship with my dad is more complicated. He's not really in the picture, and I think that's made me a little reckless, always jumping into things to fill that void. But also, there's a part of me that's really resilient, you know? Like I can handle anything because I've already handled the worst.

I think that's where the comedy comes in. It's my way of making sense of everything. Of filling in the blanks. I don't know if that makes sense, but it's the best I've got.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Huh. Five words. Five words about Mama. Five words about the woman who sang gospel in the kitchen while chopping onions, who taught me to read by pointing at street signs, who never once said "I love you" out loud but showed up to every single one of my middle school plays even when she was exhausted.

Where to start? Protective, I guess. Yeah, that's one. She was always watching out for me, making sure I was safe, you know? Even when she was tired.

Loving, definitely. But that feels too obvious. It's not like we ever said it. Maybe "unspoken." That's a good one. Unspoken love. That's us.

Firm. Oh, she was firm. No nonsense. If I stepped out of line, she'd give me that look—the one that could freeze lava. But it was fair. Always fair.

Patient. God, was she patient. All those nights I'd stay up too late reading, and she'd still get me ready for school in the morning without a word of complaint.

And the last one... maybe "strong." She had to be. Raising me alone, teaching, singing, keeping our little family together. She was a force.

But why am I even thinking about this? It's not like it's a big deal. Just five words. But it feels... heavy. Like maybe I'm not ready to say these things out loud. Or maybe I am. Maybe it's good to say them. To hear them.

But what if I choose the wrong words? What if I leave something important out? Or put something in that doesn't quite fit? It's just so... vulnerable.

Stop overthinking it, Jamie. It's just an exercise. It's not like it's a test. Just say the words. Let them write them down. See what happens.

But what if... what if it opens something up? What if it makes me feel things I don't want to feel right now? I'm not sure I'm ready for that.

Then again, maybe I should just go for it. Take the chance. See where it goes. It's not like I have anything to lose.

You're overcomplicating this. Just pick the words. Say them. Move on. You got this.
response_text:
Okay, let me think. Umm... I'd say "protective." "Unspoken" — as in love. "Firm." "Patient." And "strong." Yeah, those five. Let's see why.

"Protective" because she always had my back. "Unspoken" because, you know, we weren't big on saying "I love you" out loud. "Firm" because she didn't put up with any nonsense. "Patient" — Jesus, the woman had the patience of a saint. And "strong" because she had to be, raising me on her own. Yeah, those feel right.

She was the one who taught me to dance to juju music while she cooked. She'd laugh and say, "Oya, jamie, move those hips like you mean it!" Even if I was just standing there shaking my head. She made everything a performance.

 elephant in the room, though, isn't she? Not just for me. For my mother too. That's why Mom never talks about her. Too much there. But it's okay, I can talk about her. I don't mind. I can keep it light. A story, maybe. The time she caught me trying to steal plantains from the kitchen and chased me around the table. Classic stuff.

I should say something. They're waiting. Just a nod. A simple, "Yes, my grandmother." That's enough for now. Let them ask if they want more. I can give it. I want to give it. But not all at once. Not yet.

What if they don't follow up? What if they just move on? Then I know. This was just small talk. A passing thought. But no, they wouldn't ask like that if it wasn't important. They're not the type.

I should just say it. "Yes, my grandmother." And then see what happens. Let them lead. I can follow. For now.

Jokingly?

...

Actually, maybe this is interesting. They're trying to see where I come from. Understand the pressure that made me. Could be a good thing. Maybe I should lean into it.

But how much do I really want to share? We're still new, right? Not sure I want to lay all my family baggage out yet.

...

Play it cool. Deflect a little. Tell the truth, but not too much. See how they react.

Or just say "not really" and move on. They don't need my whole life story.

But then they might think I'm shutting down. And I don't want that.

Maybe this is a test. See if I can be vulnerable.

But vulnerable is one thing, oversharing is another.

...

Damn, why is this so complicated? It's just a question.

I think I can do this. Just breathe. Answer honestly.

But first, let them know I'm thinking about it. Show I'm engaged.

"Hmm, that's an interesting question."

Yeah, that's a good start. Then... what?

"Honestly, my mom could be pretty intense, but I never felt threatened. Just... accountable."

See, that works. Short, sweet, gives them something without going too deep.

Unless they push for more. Then I'll deal with that when it comes.

For now, let's see where this goes.

Okay, five words. Five words for the years when I was still small enough to sit on her lap. When I was still small enough to sit on her lap, but old enough to notice things. What sticks? What lingers? What words would I pick that wouldn't make me sound like a Hallmark card or a therapy case study?

I could say "loving," but that's too easy. "Creative," because she always had music in her hands, in her voice, in the way she moved through the world. "Tired," because she was always tired, but never too tired for me. "Proud," because she'd beam when I did something clever, like that time I made Mrs. Henderson laugh so hard she had to sit down.

But "proud" feels too neat. Maybe "complicated." That's one. "Complicated" because it wasn't all sunshine and giggles. There were days when she'd come home from school and just sit in the dark, and I'd bring her a glass of water and she'd pat my head like I was the one taking care of her.

"Loud" — not just in volume, but in presence. She filled a room, my mother. Even when she was quiet, she was loud. And "gentle." Because when she held me, it was like being wrapped in a soft blanket, even if her hands were rough from playing the piano all day.

Five words. I can do this. But why do I feel like I'm standing at the edge of something deep?
response_text:
Hmm, okay, let's see. "Loud," definitely. Not just her voice—her presence. She could walk into a room and just... fill it, you know? Like when she sang in the choir, her voice would just swallow the whole church.

"Creative." Always had music in her hands, in her soul. She'd hum while cooking, tap rhythms on the table. The woman made life into a sonata.

"Gentle" — but not in a soft way. More like a warm blanket. Even when she was tired, she'd hold me like I was something precious.

"Tired." Can't lie, she was always tired. But never too tired for me. That's the thing, she'd be dead on her feet after teaching all day, but she'd still sit and listen to me go on about whatever stupid thing I'd done at school.

"And... 'complicated.' Because it wasn't all sunshine. There were days she just sat in the dark, and I'd bring her water, and she'd pat my head like I was the one taking care of her. But that's part of it, too, right? The good and the hard, all mixed up.

response_text:
Reliable. Disciplined. Respected. Tired. Proud.

My father was always there. He ran a tight ship at home just like he did at the mill. People listened to him—including me. He worked himself into the ground but always found time to ask about my grades. Case closed. What else do you need to know?

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Now, here? In the middle of a crowd? Not exactly the time or place I'd pick to dive into the depths of my childhood trauma. But she's asking, and there's something earnest about the way she holds the pen, ready to capture my words. Five adjectives. Five little windows into the void of a man who left more silence than memory.

I want to say distant. That's the easy one. Then maybe neglectful. But is that fair? He wasn't around, but whose fault was that? Mine? His? The world's cruel joke?

I could say absent. But that's just restating the obvious. Maybe mysterious? No, that's putting a sparkle on something that wasn't there.

What's the fifth one? Angry? But I don't remember him being angry. Just gone.

This is harder than I thought. Maybe I should make a joke, lighten the mood. But she's watching me, waiting, and there's something sincere in her eyes.

I wonder if she's probing, trying to understand me better. Or is this just a therapeutic exercise? Either way, it's making me think.

Should I say "unreliable"? That's a strong one. Or "unknown"? To me, he was a blank space in the family photo.

I need to be honest, but I don't want to sound bitter. I mean, I'm not bitter. Just...

OK, I can do this. Let's go with "distant" first. That's the safest. Then "neglectful." Ouch. But it's true.

What's the third one? "Uninvolved." Yes, that fits. He was like a shadow, always there but never really present.

Fourth one? "Confusing." Because I never knew where I stood with him.

Fifth? "Silent." He spoke in absences, in letters that never came, in phone calls that were never made.

There. Five words. Now, how do I explain them without sounding like I'm rehashing old wounds?

I can do this. I can be open. She seems like she can handle it. But what if I start crying? Or worse, what if I don't feel anything at all?

No, I need to focus. This is about understanding, not about performing. She's not the audience here.

But what if I say too much? What if I give away too much of myself?

No, it's okay. She's asking for honesty, and I can do that. I can be vulnerable.

But what if...

Just start with the words. One at a time. I can do this.
response_text:
Ugh, you're really making me dig up some old bones here. Okay, fine. Distant, neglectful, uninvolved, confusing, and... silent. There. Happy?

Now, why did I choose them? Because that's what he was. Distant because he was never really there. Neglectful because he didn't stick around to see what he left behind. Uninvolved because he didn't care enough to be. Confusing because I never knew what to expect from him. And silent because he never said what he meant, or meant what he said. If he said anything at all.

But hey, it's not like I'm bitter or anything. Just stating facts.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Okay, I can't just deflect with a joke this time, can I? ...Not that you'd let me escape that easily. I wonder if you're trying to peek behind the curtain, or if this is just... genuine curiosity. I mean, I *know* it's genuine, but... maybe I don't *know*. God, why does that even matter? You're asking about my parents. That's a big ask. Not because it's painful, really. It's just... a lot.

My mother's the one who raised me. She's the one who taught me to tell a joke right—pacing, timing, the pause before the punchline. But she's also the one who... well. She's the one who taught me how to survive a room when the air goes out. My father? Well. He's a name we don't say. Literally. We just don't. So what's that say about me? Maybe I'm great at saying nothing. Maybe I'm great at filling the silence.

But you're not asking for my set, Jamie. You're asking for... what? The roots? The dirt under my nails? I don't know. They taught me to laugh, to create, to survive. Is that enough? Do I want to tell you about the Sundays I'd watch her direct that choir, the way she could make a hundred voices move as one? Or the way she'd sigh and rub her temples when the rent was late and the ramen was all we had? ...You didn't ask for a memoir. But maybe you did. Shit.

Is this a talk you want, or a story? I can do both. But I think—no, I *want*—to give you the story. The clean one. The funny one. The one with the punchline. Even if it's not the whole truth. Can I get away with that? Or will you keep asking? God, I hope you do. That means you care. That means—fuck, I don't know what it means. It means something good.

What was it like? Distant. He was always tired. Always working. Always coming home smelling like the mill. Like sweat and metal and something else—something acrid. Like the air in the town. Like the water.

Strict. That's another one. He had rules. Lots of them. And punishments. Not physical. Not like that. But he could freeze a room with a look. With silence. With that disappointed sigh.

Proud. Yeah, that's in there too. He was proud of me. Of my grades. Of my chess. Of the scholarship. He didn't say it much, but I could tell. He'd pat my shoulder. Grunt. That was enough.

Absent. That's the one they want to hear, isn't it? The one that fits the narrative. The one that makes me sound like some damaged thing. But it's not that simple. He was there. Just... not there. Does that make sense? No. Of course it doesn't. That's why I don't do this. Words fail.

But I don't know how to do that. I don't know how to talk about feelings. I don't even know if I have any. Or if I do, I don't know how to access them. They're like a foreign language I once studied but have long since forgotten.

But she's still looking. Waiting. Expecting. And I can't just walk away. Not from this. Not from her. Because if I do, I'm the one who's rejecting. And that's not who I am. That's not what I do.

But what do I say? What do I tell her? The truth is, I don't know. I don't remember feeling rejected. Or if I did, I don't remember it. Or maybe I just don't want to remember it. Because remembering it would mean feeling it. And feeling it would mean I'm not in control. And if I'm not in control, then what's the point?

But she's still waiting. And I can't just stand here. I have to say something. Anything. Even if it's a lie. Even if it's just a distraction. Even if it's just a way to buy time.

But I don't want to lie to her. Not about this. Not about anything. Because if I lie, then I'm not me. And if I'm not me, then what's the point?

But I don't know what the truth is. And I don't know how to find out. And I don't know how to tell her that. Because if I tell her that, then I'm admitting defeat. And I don't admit defeat. Not ever.

But she's still looking. And I'm still here. And I have to say something. So I'll say the only thing I can say. The only thing that makes sense. The only thing that's true.

But she's still looking. And I'm still here. And I don't know what to do. But I know I can't just stand here forever. So I'll say something else. Something more. Something that might make sense.

"I don't remember feeling rejected. Not really. I mean, I know there were times when I felt left out, or ignored, or whatever. But I don't think I ever really felt rejected. Not in the way you're talking about. Not in the way that matters."

But she's still looking. And I'm still here. And I don't know if it's enough. But it's all I've got. And it's better than nothing. Better than a lie. Better than silence.

Age 5 to 12. That's when he was still... normal. Before the mill started getting to him. Before he got grumpy all the time.

Distant. That's one. He was always busy. Always working. Even when he was home, he was tired. And he didn't like to talk much.

Strict. Another one. Rules were rules. No exceptions. I remember one time, I came home late from a friend's house. He made me stand in the corner for an hour. Didn't even care that I was scared.

Hardworking. He never took a day off. Even when he was sick. I remember him coughing up blood once, but he still went to work the next day.

Proud. Yeah, he was proud. But only when I did something he thought was important. Like when I won that chess tournament. He didn't say much, but I could see it in his eyes.

And the last one... I don't know. Maybe disciplined. He believed in structure. Everything had its place. Nothing was ever left to chance.

But why am I even thinking about this? It's just a question. Just five words. It shouldn't matter this much.

Still, I can't help but feel like this is a trap. Like they're trying to catch me off guard. I don't like feeling vulnerable.

But if I don't answer, they might think I'm hiding something. And that's worse. I need to stay in control.

Okay. I can do this. I'll choose the words. I'll explain them. And then I'll move on.

But I won't let them see how much this bothers me. I'll keep it together. I always do.
response_text: Frankly, I don't see the point of this exercise. But if I must: distant, strict, hardworking, proud, and disciplined. That should cover it. I don't need a minute to think about it.

This is hard in a way I didn't expect. It's not like I'm not used to talking about him. I've done bits about him bomb onstage. But that was different. That was performance, not therapy. Not education.

Absent. That's another. Not just physically, but like his mind was always somewhere else. Even when he was there, he wasn't there.

My chest feels tight. Is it okay to say "confusing"? Because that's what it was. One minute he'd be there, the next he'd be gone for months. No warning, no explanation.

I'm supposed to keep going. What else? Maybe "unreliable". I didn't learn to trust easily because of him.

Is this too much? They said they wanted to know. This is knowing, right? Even if it hurts a little.

I think I'll say the words. Five words. Distant. Absent. Confusing. Unreliable.

Why did I choose them? Because they're true. Because they're the words that come to mind when I think about him. Because it's hard to think of anything else.

Why those? Because, like, imagine building a person in your head who’s mostly made of silence and half-remembered fights through glass doors. You want to know why I’m like this? There you go. He was a goddamn ghost.

monologue_text:
Damn. That’s specific. Childhood relationship with my father? Since I was five? Like digging through the basement for old records I haven’t looked at in years. Five adjectives? That’s arbitrary. Why five? Why not three or seven? She made it five. Fine. But why does this even matter?

I don’t do this. Not with anyone. My father’s dead. What’s the point of digging this up? It’s not like it changes anything. I already know what I need to know about him. About me.

But she’s watching. Waiting. Pencil poised, like this is some kind of test. I hate tests I haven’t prepared for. Especially ones where the criteria are unclear. What’s she looking for here? Insight? Emotional depth? Some kind of psychological revelation?

I could just give her generic words. "Supportive." "Strict." "Present." But that feels like cheating. Or lying. And I don’t lie. Not about things that matter. Is this one of those things that matter? I don’t know.

But if I say the right words, maybe this speeds up whatever this is supposed to accomplish. Then I can get back to work. The marathon isn’t going to run itself.

But what are the right words? "Distant." That’s honest. He was always working. Even when he was home, his mind was somewhere else. "Expectant." That one stings. He wanted so much from me. More than I think he even knew.

"Proud." Yeah. He was proud. But in that quiet way of his. Like he wasn’t sure if he should be.

"Tired." Always tired. The mill wore him down. I wonder if I’ll feel the same way about my work when I’m his age. If I make it that far.

And the fifth one? "Gone." Because he’s been gone for years now. And some days, it still feels like he just stepped out for a minute.

But do I say that? Do I just hand her these words and let her write them down like some kind of inventory? What’s the point of this exercise? Is she trying to build a profile? Figure out how damaged I am?

Honestly, I’d rather be running. The predictability of the pavement, the rhythm of my breath. That makes sense to me. This? This is just noise. But if I want to keep moving forward, I guess I have to wade through it.

Fine. Let’s just get it over with. But I’m not doing this again.
response_text: Frankly, this feels like a waste of my time. But fine. Distant. Expectant. Proud. Tired. Gone. There.

"Present" — despite the tiredness, she was there. Never missed a parent-teacher night, even when she was exhausted. Always at my school plays, even the ones I forgot about until the last minute.

Why does "tired" feel like it belongs? It's not a nice word, but it's true. And I don't want to lie about it, but I don't want to make her sound sad. It's just... fact. She was always giving, always doing. I wonder if she'd be proud of me for being honest.

This invitation to dredge it all up again. To analyze. To dissect. To explain. As if it matters. As if it changes anything. The engine doesn't stop. The engine doesn't slow down. I could tell you about the time Mom forgot to pick me up from that sleepover because of a parent-teacher conference. I was eight? Nine? Mom cried when she finally got there. Said she was an idiot. Said she loved me. That was... was that rejection?

I remember the day I realized Dad wasn't coming back. That was a feeling. Not like sad, but like... like the world had a hole in it where something should've been. Like I was waiting for a bus that wasn't on the schedule anymore.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Five words. Five little windows into that time. Where do I even start? It's not like I've got a neat little box labeled "Mom, Ages 5-12" in my brain. It's all just... there. The smell of her perfume when she'd pick me up from school. The way her hands moved when she conducted the choir. The sound of her voice when she was tired, when she was laughing, when she was singing. The way she'd look at me when I'd make her laugh.

Five words. Okay, okay. Warm. She was always warm. Not just physically, though she was that too. She had this way of making you feel like you were the only person in the room. Safe. That's another one. Even when things were tough, even when money was tight, I always felt safe with her. Patient. God, she was patient. I was a mess as a kid, always talking, always moving, always testing boundaries. And she never lost her cool. Well, almost never.

What else? Creative. She was always creating something—music, art, stories. She made the world feel bigger, like there was always more to discover. And... and what's the fifth one? Strong. Yeah, strong. Not just physically, though she was that too. She was the kind of strong that comes from knowing who you are and not letting anyone tell you otherwise.

But why these words? Why not others? There are so many. Loving, yes, but that's a given. Protective, but that's part of safe, isn't it? Nurturing, but that's in warm. It's like trying to describe a painting with just five colors. You can't capture the whole thing, but you can get close. You can give someone a sense of it.

And why is this so hard? It's not like I haven't thought about my mom before. But putting it into words, into just five words... it feels like I'm reducing her, reducing us. But maybe that's the point. Maybe it's about finding the essence, the core of it all.

And what about the why? Why did I choose these words? Because they're true, obviously. But also because they're the words that make me feel something. They're the words that bring her to life in my mind. They're the words that make me smile, make me feel warm, make me feel safe.

But what if I choose the wrong words? What if I leave something important out? What if I can't explain it properly? It's not like I can just say "because" and leave it at that. They're asking for more, for deeper. And I want to give that to them. I want to share this part of me, this part of my past.

But it's scary too. It's opening up a part of myself that I don't always let people see. It's inviting them into a space that's just mine, just ours. But maybe that's okay. Maybe that's what this is about. Sharing, connecting, understanding.

And what about the other words? The ones I didn't choose. The ones that are just as true, just as valid. Do I have to explain why I didn't choose them? Or is it enough to say that these are the ones that came to mind first, the ones that feel right in this moment?

And what about the memories that come with these words? The good ones, the bad ones, the ones in between. Do I share those too? Or do I keep them to myself, tucked away in the corners of my mind?

But maybe it's okay to share. Maybe it's okay to let someone else in, to let them see the parts of me that are shaped by my past, by my mom, by our relationship. Maybe it's okay to be vulnerable, to be open, to be honest.

But what if they don't understand? What if they can't relate? What if they judge me, or my mom, or our relationship? What if they think less of me because of it?

But that's a risk I have to take, isn't it? If I want to connect with someone, if I want to let them in, I have to be willing to take that risk. I have to be willing to be open, to be honest, to be vulnerable.

And maybe, just maybe, it'll be worth it. Maybe they'll understand. Maybe they'll relate. Maybe they'll see me, see my mom, see our relationship, and they'll appreciate it. They'll value it. They'll cherish it.

But for now, I just have to choose the words. Five words. Five little windows into my past, into my relationship with my mom. Five little steps towards connection, towards understanding, towards honesty.

And maybe, just maybe, that's enough.
response_text:
Warm, safe, patient, creative, strong. Five quick ones. Let me think why.

Warm 'cause she was always hugging, always had that "come sit with me" energy. Safe 'cause even when we had nothing, I never felt like we were alone. Patient 'cause Jesus, I was a tornado kid and she never lost it. Creative 'cause she made everything feel like an adventure. And strong 'cause she raised me mostly by herself and never complained.

Why these? 'Cause they're the ones that pop right to the front when I think of her. They're the backbone, you know? The structure. The rest is all details.

monologue_text:
Childhood with Ma? Where to even start. *Five adjectives.* Okay, first one's easy—*loving*, definitely loving. She was always hugging, kissing my cheeks, calling me her "little sunshine." Even when I was being a brat.

But what else? *Stable.* That's important. She kept us fed, kept the lights on, kept everything... normal. Even when her paychecks were late, or when she'd come home from the school exhausted, she never let me see the cracks. *Dedicated.* She worked two jobs just to give me that stability. Band teacher by day, choir conductor by night, and still found time to cook, to help with homework, to—

Wait, is *strict* the right word? Or is that too harsh? She had rules, sure, but they were... fair. *Clear.* That's it. Her rules were clear, her expectations were clear. Even when I didn't like it, I always knew where I stood with her.

Hmm, what's the fifth one? *Proud.* Yeah, that's it. She was proud of me, always. Even when I wasn't doing anything special, she'd look at me like I'd just won an award. Like I was something extraordinary.

Why these words? Because they're true. Because they're the foundation of who I am. Because.... because I don’t want to cry right now.

*They’re just words, Jamie. Breathe.*

...Is *loving* enough? It feels like it should be more. But no, the others are important too. They all fit. They all matter.

*Just say them. They’ll understand.*

...Or maybe I should add *funny.* She had this dry wit, you know? Always cracking jokes, even on her worst days. Maybe that’s where I get it from.

*Focus, Jamie. Stick to five.*

Okay, five it is. Loving, stable, dedicated, clear, proud. That’s it. That’s the story of us.

...Now they’re going to write them down. And then what? What’s next? More questions? Deeper ones?

*You used to do this in therapy. You can handle it.*

But it’s different when it’s not just you and some shrink in a sterile office. This is—this is... more intimate, somehow. They’re really *seeing* me with this.

*And that’s what you wanted, right? Connection. Laughter. Understanding.*

But what if they don’t get it? What if they don’t *like* what they see?

*Too late now.*

Just... say the words. Let them write. See what happens next.
response_text: Loving, stable, dedicated, clear, and proud. Those are the words. They just... they cover it. She was all of those things, you know? And more, probably, but those are the ones that stick.

Stop overthinking. It's a simple question. What's one thing? Efficiency. I learned efficiency. How to get things done without wasting time or resources. How to make sure nothing gets in the way of what needs to get done.

Is that what they want to hear? It's not tragic, but it's honest. And it's not like I'm opening up some deep emotional wound. It's just a fact. I learned to be efficient.

But why are they asking? What's their angle? Are they trying to see if I'm vulnerable? If I have some soft spot they can exploit? That's ridiculous. I don't have soft spots.

I need to stay focused. This isn't about them. It's about me. And I have a job to do. A life to build. I don't have time for games.

But if I don't answer, they'll think I'm hiding something. And that's worse. So, I need to give them something. Something simple. Something that makes sense.

Efficiency. That's it. That's the answer. It's straightforward. It's honest. And it's not giving away anything important.

But why do I feel like I'm giving away something important? It's just a damn question. Why does it feel so... exposed?

Because it is. It's personal. And I don't do personal. I do business. I do numbers. I do things that make sense.

But I can't just ignore the question. I need to answer. I need to give them something. And then I need to move on.

Efficiency. That's the answer. I learned efficiency. And that's all they need to know.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?

Parents. The word alone is a trigger. Not in a dramatic way, just a fact. They were who they were. Blue-collar, practical, not given to introspection. Father's hands were always rough, smelled of machine oil. Mother's hands were softer, but just as busy—grading papers, making dinner, patching knees. They loved me in their way, I suppose. This question is too broad. It's like asking how the ocean affects the shoreline. You'd need a lifetime to answer properly. And yet, I can feel the expectation hanging in the air. They want a response, not a dissertation.

I don't do well with vague questions. They waste time. But I can't say that. It would sound like an evasion, and I don't evade. I confront. But confronting this... it's not about confrontation. It's about understanding. And understanding is a luxury I don't often afford myself.

Maybe they're looking for vulnerability. But vulnerability is a weakness, and I don't do weakness. Not in myself, not in others. It's not that I'm heartless. It's that I'm efficient. Emotions complicate things. They slow you down.

But perhaps that's the point. Maybe they're trying to see if I can slow down. If I can step outside the engine room for a moment. The thought is almost amusing. Almost.

I need to say something. But what? The truth is too complex. A simplification would be a lie. And I don't lie. Not even by omission.

Oh, this is a deep one. Five words for my father? I barely have five words for him in the present. But okay, let's see. Early childhood. Five to twelve. That's when I was still waiting for him to show up. Still hoping. Before I learned the pattern.

Absent. That's the first one. Obvious, but true. He wasn't there. Not physically, not emotionally. A ghost in the house my mother paid for.

Unreliable. That's two. Promises made, promises broken. Birthdays missed, holidays skipped. The phone ringing once in a blue moon, then silence again.

Confusing. That's three. Because he wasn't all bad. There were moments. Laughing at his jokes, feeling special when he paid attention. But then he'd disappear, and I'd be left wondering what I did wrong.

Distant. Four. Even when he was there, he wasn't really there. Like he was always somewhere else in his head. Like I was an afterthought.

And the fifth? Maybe... indifferent. That's the hardest one to say. Because it means admitting that I didn't matter to him. That I wasn't enough to make him stay, to make him try.

But why am I even thinking about this? It's not like it's a happy memory. But maybe that's why the partner wants to know. To understand where I'm coming from. To see the cracks in the foundation.

And I do want to tell them. I want to share this part of me. But it's scary. It's like opening up a wound that's been scabbing over for years. But maybe that's the point. Maybe healing starts with the truth.

No, no, no. I'm overthinking this. It's just a question. A simple, straightforward question. They're not asking for my life story. They're just trying to understand me better. And isn't that what I want too? To be understood?

So, what did I do when I was upset as a child? I drew. I laughed. I cried under the covers. I survived. And maybe, just maybe, that's enough.

Parents. The word itself is a trigger. Not in a dramatic way—no childhood trauma to unpack, no dramatic revelations. Just... facts. But what does that have to do with anything? I'm not here to dredge up the past. I'm here to move forward. The past is a fixed point, a starting line. What matters is the race, the climb, the relentless push toward the next milestone. That's what defines me, not some half-remembered conversations from childhood.

Still, the question lingers. How have they shaped me? I suppose if I had to answer, I'd say they taught me the value of self-reliance. You can't count on anyone else to carry you. You have to be the engine. Always the engine.

But why am I even considering this? It's a waste of mental energy. I should be focusing on the deal, on the numbers, on the next move. Not on some introspective exercise. Yet, here I am, thinking about it. That's the real question, isn't it? Why does this matter now?

I can see her in me sometimes—like the way I light up a room when I'm on, that's all her choir-directing energy. But I also rebelled against some of her stuff. Like, she was all structure and discipline, and look at me, barely on time for my own gigs.

Theo asked me this once, actually. He thought I was too self-deprecating on stage, that I used humor as a shield. Maybe he had a point. Mom was always so serious about everything, except when she was singing. Maybe I'm just trying to bring more of that joy into the world.

But Dad? That's a whole other story. Never around, never really wanted to be. I don't know how much that shaped me. Do I even know how to be a son, let alone a partner? Maybe that's why Theo and I didn't work out. I don't know how to do this thing right.

But hey, maybe my parents gave me the best gift by not being perfect. Look at me, I'm out here making people laugh, trying to find the funny in everything. That's gotta count for something.

How much do I want to share, though? This person's just meeting me. Do I come off as too intense if I go deep right away? But if I don't, do I seem shallow? Ugh, why does this have to be so complicated?

Maybe I should just laugh it off, make a joke. That's what I do best, right? But what if they think I'm dodging the question? I don't want to seem like I'm hiding.

I wonder if they have any idea how much this question means. It's not just about my parents, it's about me, about who I am. It's a big question. But maybe that's the point. Maybe they're trying to get to know the real me.

I should say something genuine. But what? There's so much to say, and yet, I don't know where to start. Maybe I should just be honest. Tell them about Mom, about her impact. That's a safe place to start.

But what if they don't get it? What if they don't see the connection? I don't want to feel like I'm talking to a brick wall.

Oh, screw it. I'll just wing it. That's what I do best, anyway.
response_text:
Oh, man, where do I even start? My mom—that woman was a force of nature. She raised me on her own, taught music at my school, conducted the choir on Sundays. I got my love of performing from her, no doubt. She was all about structure and discipline, but when she sang? Pure joy. I can see her in my stand-up, in how I try to bring that lightness to the stage. Though I might be a little more of a mess than she ever was.

My dad, on the other hand? That's a whole other story. He wasn't around much, and I don't think he really wanted to be. I don't know how much that shaped me, honestly. Maybe it's why I'm always looking for that missing piece, you know? But hey, maybe their imperfections are what made me who I am today. I'm out here making people laugh, finding the funny in everything. That's gotta count for something, right?

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text: Hmm, parents. Always the big question. I mean, I know what I say on stage, but what do I really think? Mom was everything—band teacher, choir director, my whole world. Dad? Ghost. Literally. Like, he's not even a memory, just a name they don't say. But Mom, she was there, you know? Always singing, always making sure I had a joke ready for the kids at school. I guess that's where the comedy comes from. But also, I wonder if I'm just performing for her approval, even now. Not that she'd ever ask for it. She's too busy being proud. But what does that even mean? Proud of what? The jokes? The bar gigs? The fact that I'm still in Brooklyn, chasing this dream? Or is she just proud I'm alive? God, I hope it's not that. That's too heavy for a Tuesday. But hey, at least I'm not crying on the train anymore. That's progress, right? But what does that have to do with who I am now? I mean, I'm here, I'm talking, I'm... what? What am I even saying? I should probably say something. But what? Just keep it light. Always keep it light.
response_text: You know, my mom was this powerhouse—band teacher by day, choir director by Sunday. Always singing, always making sure I could make someone laugh. So I guess that's where the need to perform comes from. As for my dad... well, he's the guy we don't talk about. But Mom, she gave me this weird combo of confidence and the constant fear I'm not enough. On stage, off stage, doesn't matter. But hey, look at this—still standing. Not sure if that's her or just my own stubbornness. Probably a mix.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Ugh, parents. Where to start. They asked for the whole parade and I’ll give them the whole parade, but maybe just the fun float with the dancing clowns, not the slow part where the elephants trudge by. Mom’s been gone five years now. Still smells like her in the old apartment sometimes, that lavender stuff she used to put on her wrists. Dad’s been gone longer, but in a different way. Absence like a shadow that never quite falls the same on the same patch of ground.

They want the whole story? Fine. But which one? The one where I learned to make people laugh because it’s easier than explaining why I’m sad? Or the one where I turned out okay despite it all? Or the one where I’m still waiting for my mother to come back from Lagos?

Maybe I’ll tell the funny one. The one where I’m the lovable screw-up who turns every tragedy into a punchline. Safe. Clean. Applause. But then again, they did say “in general.” Maybe they want the unvarnished truth. Maybe they want the raw stuff.

But why am I overthinking this? It’s a question. A simple question. And here I am, building a whole set around it. Maybe it’s because it’s been a while since someone asked me something like this. Something real. Something that isn’t about the next gig or the last gig or the rent.

I could tell them about Mom’s hands, how they always smelled like chalk and cedar. About the way she’d hum off-key while she cooked and how I’d join in, deliberately singing worse just to hear her laugh. About the way she’d sit me down every Sunday and say, “Jamie, you’re going to make something of yourself, mark my words.”

And Dad? What about Dad? The ghost in the room. The name we don’t say. The man who left and never looked back. The man who taught me what it means to be absent.

But do I want to go there? Do I want to dig all that up? Maybe I’ll just give them the highlights. The sanitized version. The one that ends with me on stage, making people laugh, feeling alive.

Or maybe I’ll surprise myself and tell the truth. The whole truth. The messy, complicated, beautiful truth. After all, isn’t that what this is all about? Connection. Real connection. The kind that doesn’t come with a punchline.
response_text: Whoa, heavy question. Let me put my bartender cap on to figure out which alcohol is gonna help this conversation. Honestly? I think they made me a bit of a chameleon. My mom was all about structure and routine—she taught band, so you can imagine—but also had this wild sense of humor. Meanwhile, my dad was more like a shadow in the room. So I learned to adapt, you know? Make people laugh to fill the silence, and give a damn good performance to prove I’m worth the space. But hey, at least I can work a room now—literally and figuratively.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Okay, childhood. That's a big question. I've been asked about my childhood at open mics before, but this feels different. This isn't just for a joke. They're actually asking what I learned.

I mean, I know what's expected here, right? Everyone wants to hear about the struggling artist origin story, the hardship that made me who I am. But that's not really how it was. My mom worked hard, yes, but it wasn't all struggle. There was music in our house all the time. She'd play her old records, and I'd dance around the kitchen. That's where I got my love of performance, I think. Not from some deep trauma, but from joy.

But do I say that? It feels too... simple. Too positive. People want complexity. They want to understand the "why" behind the person. And I do have that. The thing with my dad, for example. But that's not what they're asking. They're asking about what I gained.

Maybe I should tell them about the laughter. How my mom's laugh was this big, booming thing that could fill a room. How she'd laugh at my jokes even when they weren't that good. That's where the comedy came from, I think. Not from some need to be loved, but from a genuine love of making people laugh.

But I don't want to sound like I'm bragging. Or worse, like I'm making excuses. I had a good childhood. That's not something to apologize for. But it's also not something I usually lead with.

I wonder if they're fishing for something specific. Maybe I should ask them what they mean. But that might come off as defensive. I don't want to seem like I'm hiding anything.

Maybe I should just be honest. Tell them that the biggest thing I learned was that joy is a choice. That even when things are tough, you can find something to laugh about. That's what my mom taught me, anyway.

But I don't want to sound preachy. I'm not here to give a TED talk. I'm here to connect. To share a part of myself.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Hmm, five words? Early childhood. Mom and me. Okay, so not just any five words, words that really hit it. Not just "loving" or "strict" — those are easy. What's under that? What do I feel when I think about her from back then?

The way she'd sing "Count on Me" by Bruno Mars in the kitchen while cooking. Always humming. Always making sounds. Even when she was mad.

"Musical" could be one. Or "melodic"? No, "musical" feels right. That's definitely one.

Then what else? She was always... there. Not just physically, but like, present. "Present" is too obvious. "Attentive"? No, that's not quite it. She was... engaged. Even when she was tired. Even when I was being a little shit.

"Engaged" might work. Yeah, that feels true.

Then what else? She was... proud. Not in a boastful way, but like, she believed in me. Even when I was scared to try anything new. "Believing"? No, "proud" is better.

Then what's fourth? Hmm. She was... patient. Not in a saintly way, but she'd let me try things over and over. Like when I was learning to whistle. She never got annoyed. She'd just sit there and wait until I got it.

"Patient" feels right. So that's four. One more.

What's the fifth one? Something that's not just about how she was with me, but like... her essence? She was strong. Not just tough, but... resilient. She kept going, no matter what. "Resilient" feels right.

Okay, so: musical, engaged, proud, patient, resilient. That's five. They feel good. They feel true.

But explaining them? That's the tricky part. I don't want to sound like I'm giving a TED Talk. I want to be real. I want to be honest.

And it's kind of weird, thinking about Mom like this. Not in a bad way, just... it's personal. But I trust them. They asked for a reason. They want to understand.

Yeah, I can do this. I can choose the words. I can explain them. I can be real about it.

And hey, if I can make them laugh while I'm doing it, even better.
response_text:
Alright, let’s see. Early childhood with Mom? Okay, here we go:

Musical. She was always humming or singing, even when she was cooking or cleaning.

Engaged. Like, she was really there with me, even when she was tired.

Proud. Not in a braggy way, but like she believed in me before I did.

Patient. God, that woman had the patience of a saint. Especially when I was trying to learn things.

Resilient. She just kept going, no matter what happened.

There you go. Five words.

Now, explaining why I picked them? Let’s see if I can do that without getting all sappy.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text: Hmm, five words. Five words about Mama. Warm, probably. That's the first one. She was always hugging, always cooking, always singing. Even when she was tired, she'd find a way to make the house feel full. What else? Protective, definitely. She didn't let anyone mess with me, not even the other teachers at school. She'd show up at parent-teacher night with her hair done, ready to fight for me. Loving, obviously. But that's almost the same as warm. Maybe... strict? She had rules, and she enforced them. But not cruel, not harsh. Just... firm. Like, "Jamie, you will do your homework before you watch TV," kind of firm. What's the fifth one? Funny. She had this dry wit. Remember when she caught me sneaking cookies and said, "Boy, you better hope the Lord doesn't strike you down before I do"? Made me laugh every time. Okay, so: warm, protective, loving, strict, funny. That's five. Why did I choose them? Because they're true. Because they're the words that come to mind when I think about her. Because... because I want to remember her like this. Not just as my mother, but as a person. A whole person. Who loved me, but also had her own way of doing things. Who was strong, but also had a sense of humor. Who was there for me, but also let me be me. Yeah, that's it. That's why I chose them.
response_text: Alright, let's see. Warm, protective, loving, strict, funny. That about covers it. Warm because she always made our place feel like a hug. Protective because she'd go full mama bear if anyone messed with me. Loving, well, that's obvious. Strict because rules were rules, but she loved me through them. And funny, because who else would compare my cookie theft to divine wrath? That's Mama.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text: Oh, they're asking the deep question now. Where do I even start?
response_text: You know, honestly? I learned to pack a lot of life into small spaces. Queens taught me that. And that laughter is just as important as breathing. My mom was the kind of person who could find a joke in anything, even when things were tough. She’d tell me, “Jamie, always make sure you’re having a good time, even if it’s just you in the room.” And that’s stuck with me. Even when I bomb on stage, I’m still out there laughing.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text: Childhood. Upset. Those words don't pair. What does she think she's going to find there? There's nothing to find. I was fine. I was the one who kept my room clean, who did my homework without being told. The one who didn't make trouble. Maybe cried once or twice in the bathroom, quickly. But what child didn't? That's not the kind of thing you share. It's not relevant. And why is it relevant now? We're not here to talk about childhood. We're here to... What are we here to do, exactly? I should have asked that at the beginning. This isn't a peer review. It's not a due diligence meeting. Different rules. Different expectations. And I don't have the manual. Why is she asking? What's the angle? Is there an angle? Maybe she's just curious. Maybe that's all it is. But curiosity is a hunger, and I don't like being food. Focus. She asked a question. It deserves an answer. Or at least an acknowledgment. But how much do I give? Just the surface. The bare minimum. That's always been enough before. Usually more than enough. But this isn't a negotiation. It's not a pitch. It's not even a first meeting. It's a... What is it? I don't know. And I don't know how to find out. Maybe I should just tell her. Upset as a child. What did I do? I didn't get upset. Or at least, I didn't show it. That's what matters, right? What's shown, not what's felt. That's how the world works. That's how I work. But she's looking at me like there's more there. Like I'm some kind of puzzle to be solved. I don't like puzzles. I solve problems. There's a difference. Problems have solutions. Puzzles are just games. And I don't play games. Unless I can win. Can I win this? What does winning even look like here? I don't know. And that makes me want to leave. But I can't. Not yet. Not until I understand the rules. Or at least until I can make her think I do. So I'll give her something. Not too much, but enough to keep her from asking again. And then we can move on. To something else. Something safer. Something I know how to do.
response_text: Frankly, I didn't spend much time upset. There wasn't time for that. I had things to do, places to be.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text: Parents, huh? Well, that's a mic drop right there. I mean, I *know* what I'm supposed to say—how Mama's choir rehearsals taught me timing, how her quiet strength after Noname Dad vanished is why I don't flinch from loud things.But do I *want* to unpack that in front of a near-stranger? Maybe they're fishing for funny, but what if they're not? Operation: Make-Good-Impression wants me to hit the punchline: "Well, I definitely got *stage presence* from somewhere." But the truth is, I don't even... Mama *is* the reason I don't trust stillness, come to think. The way she'd pace the kitchen after the phone went quiet on her. I wonder if *they* get that. Or is this just where first dates go now? DNA deep-dive before the check arrives? God, *please* don't let this be a therapy session. Then again... if they can laugh at my story about hiding under the piano during Thanksgiving dinner, maybe this turns into something. *Or* I just sound like a broken record about abandonment. Wait. Wait. What if they think I'm *judging* my mom? I'm not. She did what she had to. But I *am* doing that thing where I talk too fast when topics get heavy. Shit. Should I pivot? Tell the one about her teaching me to juggle spoons while she graded papers? Light shines on the cheekbones now—and is that a sign? Just *ask* what their parents were like? No, no, keep it symmetric. If they asked... maybe they're trying to find out if we're the same species. *Do* I want to be understood right now? Or just entertaining? The bit about the choir accident *always* lands. But if they're looking for real... is that what I'm here to give? ...Dammit. I *hate* when two topics fight for the mic at once. Focus. Breathe. The old JO would've fired off three jokes and a shrug by now. But Theo used to *hate* when I deflected. Said I was like a jazz musician who never came in for the chorus. ...Maybe they're waiting. Stop waiting. Just... answer. Somewhere in the middle. Where it's safe.
response_text: Oh, man, where to start? My mom—she taught me to make an entrance even when I'm just walking into a room. The woman could conduct a choir with one hand and patch up a skinned knee with the other. So yeah, I think I got my timing from her, and maybe my tendency to fix things with a quick joke. My dad? Well, let's just say I learned early that if you don't see the punchline coming, you duck. But honestly, I think what stuck most was watching her keep going. That's where the stubborn optimism comes from. And the way I can't sit still for too long. That, and my love for dramatic exits.

response_text: Oh, okay, that's a fun game. Um, let me think. The first one that pops to mind is probably "loud" — not like she yelled a lot, but she's just a big presence, you know? Like, her laugh could fill a whole room. And maybe "creative" — she's a music teacher, so there was always music around, always something being made. "Determined" — she raised me alone, worked two jobs, and never complained about it. "Proud" — she was always proud of me, even when I wasn't doing much of anything. And "soft" — not in a weak way, but she had this way of holding me when I was upset that made everything feel okay. Is that five?

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Gained from my childhood. What a strange way to put it. Like it was a transaction. Did I gain anything? Or was it just deposit of burdens to carry? Chess, sure, that gave me something. But the rest? The silence in that house. Father's hands, gnarled from the mill. Mother's way of smiling even when she was tired. What did I gain? Efficiency, maybe. The ability to see what needed doing and do it before anyone else noticed. Before they could complain.

Gained. Fine. I could talk about discipline. Punctuality. The way you can't rely on anyone else to move things forward. The way you have to be the one who does. The engine. That's me. The one who keeps things running.

But do I want to say that? It sounds like an excuse. For what? Not being more... approachable. That's what she said last time. When I brushed off her comment about my "intensity." She'd used air quotes. Like it was a joke. But it wasn't.

I could say I learned to make my own path. That's true. And ambitious. Good for a first impression. But it's not about ambition, is it? It's about not wanting to end up like them. Trapped. Worn out before your time. Not that there's anything wrong with that. Just not for me.

She's waiting. Eyes on me. Interested. But not too interested. Like she's got a professional distance. Good. I can work with that. Keep it light. Keep it moving.

What did I gain? What did I gain? The ability to outthink, outwork, outlast. That's what. That's the lesson. The one that matters.

But do I want to tell her that? It's too much. Too revealing. Too much like admitting something. Admitting that I'm still running. Still trying to escape.

Maybe I should deflect. Talk about something else. Something safer. But I don't want to. I want to be seen. Not all of me, of course. But enough. Enough to matter.
response_text: Frankly, the biggest thing was learning to keep moving. You can't waste time waiting for things to happen or for other people to figure things out. If you want something done right, you do it yourself. That's the lesson.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Hmm, five words for Mom. That’s a lot. Okay, okay, let’s see. Warmth, definitely. That's the first one. She was always hugging, always feeding us, always singing. Even when she was tired from teaching all day, she’d make these big Nigerian meals. Efo riro, jollof rice, all that. The whole apartment smelled like home.

Patient, probably. Had to be, raising me alone. I remember coming home with some D minus, and she’d just shake her head and say, "Jamie, you can do better, but let’s figure it out together." Never yelled, not really. Just... steady.

Proud. Yeah, proud. She’d tell everyone I was going to be a comedian before I even knew what that meant. "My son, he’s going to make people laugh!" Like it was a done deal. That’s moms, though.

Supportive. When I got heckled at that first open mic, she was the one who said, "Try again next week." Didn’t even laugh at the stories I came home with. Just said, "You’ll get them next time."

Hmm, fifth one. That one’s tricky. Strong, maybe? Not in the physical way, but she had this quiet strength. Like when her mother died, she didn’t collapse. Just... handled it. Kept teaching, kept singing. Kept me together, honestly.

But is that the right word? Strong feels a bit... cold. Maybe "resilient." That fits, too. Or maybe "loving" for the fifth one. But that feels too broad. Love covers everything, and this is supposed to be specific. Okay, resilient. That’s the one.

What if they ask why resilient? I’ll say something about how she never let life break her. Even when it should have. Theo always said I romanticized her a little too much. Maybe he’s right. But hey, what kid doesn’t?

Why am I overthinking this? It’s just words. But it’s also Mom. And they’re writing them down. Big difference.
response_text: Oh, okay, let me think. Warmth, definitely. She was always hugging, always feeding us, always singing. Patient, yeah—that woman had the patience of a saint. Proud, always telling everyone I was going to be a comedian before I even knew what that meant. Supportive, through all the bombs and hecklers. And resilient—she never let life break her, even when it should have. You getting all that down?

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text: Huh. Threatening. That’s a word. They’re really leaning into the drama. But they’ve got the tone right—light, curious. Not digging for dirt, just trying to piece together the puzzle. But what’s this leading to? Are they building a set or just being nosy? Can’t blame them for asking. Family stuff always gets a laugh. But my parents? Mom’s got a voice that could strip paint off walls, but she’d never threaten. Not like that. Dad? Never there to form an opinion. So what do I say here? Truth? Gloss? I could spin it into a bit. But do I want to? They’re just asking. Not an interrogation. Chill, Jamie. They’re not after your family secrets. Maybe they’re just making conversation. But damn, the word ‘threatening’ is heavy. Why’d they pick that? Maybe it’s just their phrasing. Or maybe they’re trying to get under my skin. Nah, that’s paranoid. They’re just curious. But what’s the angle? What do they want to know? And why now? Focus, Jamie. They’re asking about my parents. That’s it. Not a big deal. But—why is this bugging me? Is it the word? The implication? Or just because it’s personal? I don’t mind talking about Mom. But ‘threatening’? That’s not her. Unless—maybe they’re testing me. Seeing how I react. But that’s crazy. They’re not like that. Or am I projecting? I need to relax. It’s a simple question. But—what’s the right answer? The funny answer? The honest answer? Or do I just deflect? "Oh, you know parents!" Something vague. Or—I could tell them about the time Mom threatened to take away my sneakers if I didn’t practice my piano. But that’s not threatening. That’s just Mom being Mom. God, I hate talking about family stuff. It’s like laying out all your cards on the table. But they’re not asking for vulnerability. Just a story. A bit. Something to laugh about. Or am I overthinking this? Maybe they’re just making small talk. But why this topic? Is it relevant? Or are they just fishing? Damn it, Jamie, stop spiraling. They’re asking about your parents. monologue_text:
Childhood. Learned. Childhood learned. What am I supposed to say about that? I don't even know what they're asking. What is this, a therapy session? I didn't come here to be analyzed.

Though really, what did I learn? I learned to be efficient. I learned that time is a resource to be managed, not wasted. I learned that emotions are distractions, like ads on a webpage. Annoying. I learned that if you want something done right, you do it yourself.

But that's not what they're asking, is it? They want some sentimental story about how my childhood shaped me. Well, maybe it did. But I don't see the point in dwelling on it. What's done is done.

I suppose I could talk about the chess. That's a clean story, at least. State champion at thirteen. I learned how to think ahead, how to anticipate moves. How to calculate outcomes. That's useful. But it's not like I'm still playing. I quit. I hated losing.

Or maybe they want to hear about my father. The stroke. The funeral. The efficiency of it all. But what's the lesson there? That life is unpredictable? That you have to be ready for anything? I already knew that.

Wait, maybe that's the thing. That I don't believe in meaning. That's a lesson from childhood. My family didn't understand me. They didn't get my drive, my ambition. But I'm not sure I want to say that. It sounds like I'm bragging. And I don't want to come off as arrogant. Even if it's true. Even if I am better.

I don't know. Maybe I should just tell them I don't remember. That I don't think about it. That's not entirely untrue. I don't dwell on the past. I'm focused on the future. On the next move. On the next win.

But they're looking for something more. Something deeper. Something I'm not sure I have. Or not sure I want to give.

I should say something, though. They're waiting. I can feel it. The silence is growing. I need to fill it. I need to say something.

But what? What can I say that will satisfy them without revealing too much? Without giving them too much of me. I don't want to be known. I want to be respected. I want to be admired. But not known. Not really known.

Maybe I should just deflect. Ask them a question. Turn it back on them. But that feels like a cop-out. A way to avoid the question. And I don't like avoiding things. I like facing them. Conquering them.

But this isn't a challenge to be conquered. It's a question. A personal question. And I don't like those. I don't like being personal. I like being professional. I like being efficient. I like being the best.

This is getting me nowhere. I need to just say something. Anything. Before the silence becomes unbearable. Before they think I'm not engaging. Before they think I'm not worth their time.

I could talk about my mother. About how she taught me to read before I started school. How she encouraged my love of books, of learning. How she saw something in me that no one else did. That's a nice story. A positive one. A story that shows growth, not just cold efficiency.

But it's not the whole story. It's not the truth. The truth is more complicated. The truth is messy. And I don't like messy. I like clean lines. I like clear goals. I like measurable outcomes.

I don't know. Maybe I should just be honest. Maybe I should tell them that I don't know what I learned from my childhood. That I don't think about it. That I'm too focused on the future to dwell on the past.

But that might not be what they want to hear. That might not be what they're looking for. And I don't want to disappoint them. I don't want to fail. Not here. Not now.

I need to just say something. Anything. Before the silence becomes a chasm. Before I lose this opportunity. Before I lose them.

I need to just speak. Now.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejected? I don't recall feeling rejected. I recall facts. Events. The moves people made. Chess pieces. The way my father's hands would shake after the third beer. The way my mother's face went tight when I brought home the scholarship letter. Not rejection—just distance. A chasm. They didn't know how to bridge it, so they didn't try. And why should they? I didn't speak their language. I still don't. Rejected? No. Just... separate. Always separate.

But feeling? What's the point of dwelling on feelings? Feelings are static. They don't build anything. They don't close deals. They don't make you a partner by thirty-five. And yet, here we are, talking about feelings. About childhood. About rejection. What is this, some kind of game? A test? I don't play games. I win them.

But the partner seems sincere. Or is it just a ploy? A way to get under my skin? To find something to exploit? No, that's paranoia. I don't do paranoia. I do efficiency. I do results. I do—

Why am I even thinking about this? It's a waste of time. A distraction. I have deals to close. Numbers to crunch. A marathon to train for. This—this is just noise.

But the partner is waiting. For what? For me to spill my guts? To bare my soul? Not happening. I don't bare. I build. I construct. I execute.

Rejected. The word hang in the air. It's absurd. I was never rejected. I was simply... better. Faster. Stronger. I outpaced them all. I outthink them all. I outperform them all. Rejection implies I wanted something and was denied. I never wanted anything from them. I wanted to be left alone to win. And I did.

But the partner is still looking at me. Expecting something. An answer? A reaction? A breakdown? They won't get one. I control the narrative. I control the outcome.

Yet, there's a twitch in my chest. A flicker. A ghost of something. Not grief. Not quite. More like—like the phantom pain of an amputation. The mind remembers the limb, even when it's gone. But I don't have time for phantom pains. I have work to do.

Still, the question lingers. Rejection. Childhood. What does it matter? The past is a closed chapter. A solved problem. I don't revisit problems. I move forward. I conquer new territories.

But the partner is still here. Still waiting. Maybe they need to hear something. Anything. To feel like they've made progress. Fine. I can give them that. A bone to chew on. A morsel. A distraction from the real work.

But not the truth. Never the truth. The truth is a weakness. A vulnerability. And I don't do vulnerability. I do power. I do control. I do—

Just answer the question. Shut them up. Move on. That's the efficient thing to do. The logical thing. The thing that gets me back to work.

But what to say? What can I give them that won't give too much away? A non-answer. A deflection. A red herring. That's the play. That's the move.

But the twitch in my chest persists. Annoying. Irritating. A glitch in the system. I need to ignore it. To override it. To subdue it.

Rejected. The word is still there. Taunting me. Probing me. Testing me. I won't break. I won't budge. I am the engine. I am the force. I am the—

Just answer the question. Get it over with. Then back to work. Back to the grind. Back to the life I control. The life I command.

But the twitch. The flicker. The ghost. It's still there. Still nagging. Still—

No. I won't let it win. I won't let it break me. I am stronger than that. I am better than that. I am—

Just answer the damn question.
response_text: Rejected? Not exactly. More like they were always one chess move behind. Didn't bother me. Never slowed me down.

response_text:
Oh, man. Sixth grade, Mrs. Perez’s math class. I told this joke about how her ruler was the only thing in the room that measured up, and she just lost it—like a teakettle about to explode. That’s when I knew I had to keep doing this. It’s all about making people forget their shit for a second, you know?

But if you’re asking for something deeper, I guess... watching my mom come back from Lagos after my grandma died. She wasn’t the same after that. Not broken, just quieter. I realized you can love someone so much it changes the shape of you. Not sure if that’s the kind of lesson you were looking for, though.

She was always busy, but that was just how life was. Why these words? Because they're the bones of how I remember her back then. It's not fancy, but it's honest.

There you go, doc.
