That's honest. And it's true. But it's not the whole truth. Do I owe them the whole truth? I don't even know them. But they seem nice. And they're asking. And I want to give them something good. Something that makes them smile. Or nod. Or both. Okay. I can do this. What's the best thing I learned from my childhood? That's a big question. But I can answer it. I just need to think. And not overthink. Okay. Deep breath. Let's go.
response_text: You know what? I learned how to make people laugh. Like really laugh—not just that polite chuckle you give your aunt when she tells you her bad joke. It was my superpower, my get-out-of-jail-free card. My mom would be stressing about bills or school stuff, and I’d say something stupid, and for a second, she’d forget about everything. That’s the thing I carry with me. It’s why I’m on stage now. Well, that and the fact that I have the attention span of a squirrel.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text: Rejected? As a kid? Well, there was that time in second grade when no one wanted to be my lab partner for the potato experiment, but I just assumed they all already had partners. Not exactly rejection, more like... inadequate deduction. But then again, there was the sleepover no one invited me to in fourth grade. I knew about it because Sarah told everyone to bring pajamas the day before. I didn't make a big deal out of it, but I did stay up late that night watching "Sesame Street" reruns, pretending I had somewhere to be. That stung more than a little. But was it rejection? Or just the inevitable sorting that happens when kids start choosing teams? I don't know. Maybe it's all the same thing in the end. And now they're asking me to dig this up. Why? What's the point? They're just trying to understand, sure, but understanding what? Is this leading somewhere, or are we just poking at old bruises for the sake of it? I mean, I can talk about this, no problem. I've joked about worse on stage. But there's a difference between telling a story for laughs and... and what? Unpacking it? Analyzing it? I don't know if I have the right tools for that. Maybe that's why I do stand-up instead. Easier to just make people laugh than to actually explain myself. But they're looking at me like they really want to know. Maybe I should give them something real for once. God, what if they think I'm pathetic? No, no, they wouldn't. They're not like that. They're one of the good ones. But what if I say too much and it gets heavy? I don't want to ruin whatever this is before it even starts. Maybe I'll just make a joke out of it. That's what I do best, right? But what if they think I'm deflecting? Shit, I don't know. Just breathe. It's a simple question. They're not trying to trick me or anything. They just... care? Is that what this is? Someone actually caring about what happened to me as a kid? That's... weird. Nice, but weird. Okay, okay. Maybe I can do this. But where do I even start?
response_text: Ah, you know, the sleepover no one invited me to in fourth grade still stings a little. I didn't make a fuss, just stayed up late watching "Sesame Street" reruns pretending I had somewhere better to be. Looking back, maybe it wasn't personal, just kids being kids. But at the time? Yeah, it felt like rejection.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Hmm, five words. That's a lot. I wonder what they're looking for here. It's not like I've ever had to distill my mom into adjectives before. But okay, let's see... *early childhood*, they said. So, before Dad left? Or after? I mean, he was never really around, but... okay, focus. What's the first thing that comes to mind? "Supportive," definitely. She was always there, you know? Even when she was tired, even when she had to work two jobs. "Supportive" is a good one.

And... "Strict"? Maybe. Not in a mean way, but she had rules. She believed in discipline. But it was always clear why. She wanted me to be better, not just to obey. "Nurturing" is another one. She made sure I had everything I needed, even if she didn't have much herself. She'd cook my favorite meals, even when she was exhausted. And... "Proud"? Yeah, she was always proud of me. Even when I wasn't doing great in school, she'd find something to be proud of. "Encouraging," definitely.

But five words? That's a lot. I could go on. "Loving," obviously. She's my mom, you know? But I need to pick the most important ones. Maybe "supportive," "nurturing," "proud," "encouraging," and... "resilient"? She taught me to be strong, to keep going no matter what. Yeah, those feel right. But explaining them? That's the hard part. I hope they don't expect some deep, emotional breakdown. I'm not sure I'm ready for that. But hey, it's just words, right? It's not like they're asking me to perform stand-up about it. Yet.
response_text: You know, this is actually kinda hard. But okay, let me think. "Supportive," definitely. She was always there for me, no matter what. "Nurturing" — she made sure I had everything I needed. "Proud" — she never missed a performance or a school event. "Encouraging" — she always pushed me to do better. And... "resilient." She taught me how to keep going, even when things were tough. There you go. Five words. Now, what do you want to know about them?

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. response_text: Frankly, this isn't the kind of conversation I have at the office. If you're looking for investment insights or market analysis, I'm your man. Otherwise, I'm not sure what this accomplishes. Let's keep things professional, shall we?

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Huh. That’s... specific. Threatening? My mum? Not really. She’d snap her fingers in my face if I got too loud, but that was just her way. Jokingly, maybe when I’d leave my shoes by the door—"You want me to put these in the trash with you?"—but it was all laugh-yell. Theo used to say I got my love for dramatic confrontation from her.

Wait, why are they asking this? It’s not like I’ve been opening up about my childhood. Unless they’re trying to get at something else. Maybe they’re testing the water for... something. But I haven’t even told them about Dad yet. Not that there’s much to tell.

Do they think I’m damaged? That’d be ironic. I’m the one who walks into rooms full of strangers to make them laugh. They’re the ones who asked me out after my set last week. Maybe they’re just making conversation. Or maybe they’re trying to figure out if I’m a problem before they invest. Not that it’s an investment. We’ve been on, what, three dates?

God, I hope they’re not one of those people who overanalyzes everything. I can’t deal with that. Humor is my default. I’ll just deflect. "Threatening? Nah, my mum’s weapons of choice were embarrassment and loud sighs." That’ll get a laugh. But if they keep pushing... maybe I’ll let something real slip. Just a little. See how they handle it.

But not yet. Let’s see where this goes.
response_text: Threatening? Nah, my mum's weapons of choice were embarrassment and loud sighs. The woman could make you feel like you'd just kicked a puppy just by looking at you sideways. But threatening? Please. She'd snap her fingers in my face if I got too loud, but that was her way of getting my attention, you know?

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives, huh? That's not so bad. I can do that. Early childhood... five to twelve. That's right around when I started doing impressions. Mom said I could mimic her voice as soon as I could talk. "Jamie, stop that," in her voice. She'd laugh even as she said it.

Five to twelve... that's also right around when Nne died. Mom was... different. Not that she's ever been the type to sit around crying, but after Nne died, it was like she turned into a statue. Still warm, but you couldn't squeeze it. Not that she was ever exactly squeezeable.

What's the first word that comes to mind? Loving. Definitely loving. She made sure I had everything I needed. Not just the basics, but the little things too. Remember that time I wanted those stupid Ninja Turtles action figures? She found them at a thrift store and gave them to me for my birthday. She didn't even roll her eyes when I screamed.

Proud. Yeah, proud. She'd drag me to every school event, every church play, every comedy competition. She'd sit in the front row, clapping like I'd just won an Oscar.

Strange. No, not strange. That's not fair. Protective. That's more like it. She was always looking out for me. Like that time I got into a fight with that kid Dylan in third grade. She marched right up to the principal's office and made sure he got in just as much trouble as I did.

Hmm. What else? Supportive. Always there for me. Even when I wasn't doing so well in school, she'd sit down with me and help me study. She never let me give up.

But five to twelve... that's also when she started dating again. After Nne died, she threw herself into her work and her choir. But then she met that guy, what was his name? Greg. He was okay, I guess. A little too into himself, but Mom seemed happy. For a while, at least.

Yeah, I think that's it. Loving, proud, protective, supportive, and... what's the fifth one? Maybe "busy." She was always so busy. Teaching during the day, choir practice in the evenings, and then there was all the church stuff. I was lucky if I saw her for more than an hour a day sometimes.

But that's not a bad thing. She was doing what she loved, and she made sure I had everything I needed. That's more than a lot of kids get. So, five adjectives. Loving, proud, protective, supportive, and... busy. That works.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. monologue_text:
Hmm. Dad. Five words. Right. Where to start? It’s not like I have a whole album of memories to flip through. More like a fold-out brochure with mostly blank pages. "Distal." That’s one. Felt more like a concept than a person, you know? A shadow in a story Mom told. "Absent" feels too obvious, like I’m trying to get a point across. But it’s true. Maybe "mysterious"? Like, who was he really? But that feels too generous. He wasn’t a character in a book, just a guy who wasn’t there.

Wait, is this about me or about the exercise? Why am I overthinking it? It’s just words. But it’s not “just words.” It’s words about *that*. That thing. The thing I don’t think about. Or try not to. Is this some kind of therapy trick? Are they trying to get me to dislike them? No, that’s silly. They’re just doing their job. Or is this a job where you dig into stuff like that?

Maybe I should go with "gone." Simple, straightforward. "Unknowable." That’s a good one. "Unspoken." Yeah, that fits. "Gone," "unknowable," "unspoken"... that’s three. I need two more. "Unseen." Definitely. What else? "Irrelevant" feels harsh, but it’s kind of true. He wasn’t part of my life, so in a way, he was irrelevant. But is that fair? Or am I just being defensive? This is getting complicated. Maybe I should just say "distant" again. Or "nonexistent." That’s a word, isn’t it? No, probably not.

Why is this so hard? It’s not like I’m grieving or anything. I don’t even miss him. I don’t know him. But still. It’s weird. Like I’m expected to have an opinion about a ghost. Or a silence. Or a space where something should be but isn’t. Maybe that’s it. "Empty." That’s five. "Gone," "unknowable," "unspoken," "unseen," "empty." That works.

But why do I have to explain them? What if I just say the words and leave it at that? No, they’ll probably insist. They’ll want to know why. Why can’t I just have a word and let it sit there like a stone? It’s not like I can explain it anyway. It’s just... it is what it is. And what it is is nothing. Or close to it. So what do I say?

Maybe I should just make it up. Or joke about it. But that feels wrong. This isn’t a set. This is... I don’t know what this is. A test? An experiment? A way to dignify a nothing? I don’t want to dignify it. I want to leave it alone. But I can’t. I have to say something. So I’ll give them the words. And then... then what?
response_text: Okay, let's see. Gone. Unknowable. Unspoken. Unseen. Empty. There you go. Five words.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?

Hmm, threatening? Not really. Mom had a belt, but she never used it. More like... dramatic sighs and the silent treatment. The silent treatment could go on for days. I remember hiding my report card once, the one with the D in algebra. She found it. The way she just... looked at me. Like I'd betrayed her. That was worse than any belt.

Jokingly? Mom's humor was more... sarcastic. "You want to fail out of school? Be my guest." That kind of thing. Not really threatening, just... cutting. Like she was daring me to prove her right.

Dad's side is a whole other story. But I don't think about him much. Not worth the energy.

Why are they asking this? It's a bit personal, but... okay. I guess it's a fair question if we're getting to know each other. Just... hope they don't expect me to cry about it or something. I'm not that kind of comedian. The kind that wears their heart on their sleeve. The kind that needs to be fixed.

monologue_text:
Rejected? As a kid? Well, sure, I guess. Who didn't? God, that's a heavy question. Why are they asking this? Is this some kind of therapy thing? Not that I mind, but...
I remember when I tried out for the fifth-grade talent show and forgot the words to my joke. The room went so quiet, and then Mrs. Paterson said, "That's okay, Jamie, you'll get it next time." But her face—she was trying to be nice, but I saw it. The disappointment.
But was that rejection? Or just... me messing up? Kids can be cruel, sure. Like when I got called "Mr. Frown" because my face looked like a sad potato when I concentrated. But that was more... teasing. Was it rejection? Or just kids being kids?
I don't know if I ever felt truly rejected. Maybe because I never really wanted to be part of anything that badly. I always had my mom and her jokes. She used to say, "If they're laughing with you, you're already winning." Maybe that's why.
But, God, why am I overthinking this? It's a simple question. They're not judging me. They're just asking. But why now? What's the context? Is this about me? Or them?
I should just answer honestly. What's the harm? But what if they think I'm too sensitive? Or too tough? I don't want to come off as wounded. Or invincible. Just... normal.
Maybe I should deflect with a joke. But that might feel like I'm avoiding the question. And I don't want to do that. I want to be real here.
Okay, just say it straight. I can do that. It's not a big deal. It's just a question. About childhood. About feelings.
But God, I hate talking about feelings sometimes. It's like digging up old bones. You never know what you're gonna find.
response_text: Honestly? Yeah, I guess. Who didn't? There was this one time I bombed a talent show and the whole room went quiet. Or when they'd call me "Mr. Frown". But my mom always had jokes, you know? If they're laughing with you, you're already winning.

Threatening? Not really. I mean, my mum used to say, "I'll beat you with my shoe," but she never owned any shoes that could do damage. Those old church ladies' heels? I almost want to say something about how she'd be more likely to take away my comics than raise a hand, but then I'd have to explain what comics are, and suddenly it's Ontology Hour with Mum.

Still, I wonder if that's what they mean by "gaslighting." No, no, that's not fair. She was just... exasperated. Like when I'd come home with another detention for talking too much in class. She'd say, "Jamie, one day your mouth will get you in trouble you can't talk your way out of."

But that wasn't threatening. That was just... mum. Still, maybe I should tell them that one. The mouth one. It's funny. And it's true. I was always getting into something. But was I ever scared? Nah. Mum was more likely to make me write an essay on why I was wasting my potential than raise a hand. Though, yeah, okay, there was that one time when I knocked over her favorite vase—the one Grandma brought back from Lagos—and she picked it up in pieces and just sighed so hard I thought she'd collapse. That was the closest to threatening I ever got. "I hope you're proud of yourself, young man," she said. And I felt like such a jerk.

But I can't just sit here thinking about this. They're waiting. And I need to give them something. Maybe the shoe thing. That's funny. And it's true. And it'll show them that Mum was Mum—strict but not scary. Just... exasperated. Always exasperated.

Though, I wonder if they'll think it's too light. I don't even know them well enough to know what they expect. I need to read the room. I need to... oh, screw it. I'll tell the truth. The truth is funny. The truth is my job. Right?
response_text: Nah, my mum's go-to was always, "I'll beat you with my shoe," but let's be real—her church heels couldn't smash a fly. More likely to just flail around dramatically. The closest I got to *actual* threats was her sighing so hard I thought she'd collapse after I broke her favorite vase. She just said, "I hope you're proud of yourself, young man." Cue guilt trip.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five words. About my father. Early childhood. Here we go again, digging up the old roots. Five words. Maybe even less.

Absent. That's one. It's always the first one. But is it fair? He wasn't there, sure, but that's not all of it. Not the whole story.

Distant. That's closer. Cold. No, not cold—remoteness is different from coldness. He was like a mountain peak in the next valley over. Visible, but impossible to climb.

Confusing. Why does that one keep bubbling up? Because I never knew what to expect. One day he'd be there, the next he'd vanish. Like a weather pattern I couldn't predict.

Proud. Wait, is that accurate? Or am I just wanting it to be true? There were moments, weren't there? When he'd smile at something I did, that rare, tight-lipped smile.

Fragile. No, that's not a descriptor. But I can't help but think of him that way. Fragile, like he might crumble if I asked too much. Like he was made of glass.

Okay, four words. I need one more. What's the fifth? Supportive? No, that feels like a lie. Present? But he wasn't. What about safe? No, not safe.

Invisible. That's it. Not just absent, but like he was never there at all. Like a ghost I couldn't see.

But why? Why do I need five words? What's the point? Is this some kind of therapy exercise? Am I supposed to be healing or something?

And why does it have to be about him? What about my mum? She was there. She was solid. But no, it's always about the missing piece, isn't it? The black hole at the center of the family.

Five words. Absent. Distant. Confusing. Proud. Invisible. Can I skip the explanations? Just let the words sit there, heavy and awkward.

But they want to know why. They want to know the story behind the words. And I don't know if I have the energy for that. Not right now. Maybe not ever.

But I said I'd do this. I agreed to this. So, five words. And then, I guess, the explanations. Deep breaths. Here we go.
response_text: Ugh, fine. Absent. Distant. Confusing. Proud. Invisible.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening. What an odd question. Do they think I'm some kind of... victim? Not that I would even call it *threatening*. Father's voice raising, sure, when we'd come in late from fishing, or when I'd mouth off about something I knew better. But that wasn't *threatening*—that was just how things were. You know the rules, you follow them. It wasn't about threats, it was about respect.

Jokingly? That's even more absurd. Unless they think my father was one of those... *types* who hides abuse behind laughter. No. Definitely not. Father's idea of a joke was pointing out when one of us looked funny in a new shirt. That's it.

Why are they asking this? Is this some... psychological angle? Trying to *probe* me? Or is this just some new trend among their friends—digging up childhood trauma like it's some kind of... hobby. Ugh.

I don't even know how to answer this. Do I just say "no" and move on? Or do I have to... *explain*? God, I hate explaining things. It's not like I have some sob story to unload. My parents weren't *perfect*, but they weren't monsters either. Why does everything have to be *analyzed* these days?

...Maybe they're just trying to be... what's the word. *Empathetic*? Is that what this is? Trying to connect by digging into the past. Fine. But why the past? Why not just talk about... anything else. Work. Sports. Politics. Even the weather. Anything but *this*.

...Unless this is some kind of test. To see if I'll open up. To see if I'm *capable* of opening up. Like I'm some kind of project. No, I refuse to be treated like that. I'm not some... *case study*.

...But if I just shut them down, they might think I'm hiding something. Or worse—*repressing* something. God, the last thing I need is for them to start psychoanalyzing me. Maybe I just need to give them... enough to satisfy them and move on.

Okay. Simple answer. "No, my parents weren't threatening. They were strict, but fair." That should do it. Short. To the point. No nonsense. And then I'll shift the conversation back to something... *productive*.

...But why do I even care what they think? It's not like this is a *relationship* or anything. Just a conversation. Just... talking. But still. The last thing I need is for them to get the wrong idea. That I'm some kind of... damaged goods.

No. I'm fine. Perfectly fine. I don't need anyone *fixing* me. I just need to... answer the question and move on. That's all. Simple. Easy. Done.
response_text: No, my parents weren't threatening. They were strict, but fair. What's the point of this line of questioning?

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Fine. I can do that. It's just words. But why does it matter? What's the point of this exercise? It's not like I'm going to gain any new insights. I already know what my relationship with my father was like. It was functional. Efficient. He was a man who worked hard, came home, ate dinner, maybe watched some TV, and went to bed. There wasn't much depth to it. But is that what they want to hear? Is that what they're looking for?

I could say "distant." That's accurate. He wasn't one for emotional displays. But it's also a bit harsh. Maybe "reserved." That's more neutral. "Dependable." That's a good one. He was always there, always working, always providing. "Strict." He had rules, and he expected them to be followed. "Proud." He was proud of me, in his own way. That's five. That should be enough.

But why do I have to explain them? What's the purpose of that? It's not like I'm going to reveal some deep, dark secret about my childhood. It was what it was. I turned out fine. I'm successful. I'm independent. I don't need to dredge up the past to understand myself.

But if I don't explain, they might think I'm hiding something. They might think I'm not taking this seriously. And I do take it seriously. I take everything seriously. That's who I am. But this... this feels like a waste of time. I could be working. I could be reading. I could be doing something productive.

But no, here I am, thinking about adjectives to describe a relationship that ended when I was twenty-six. A relationship that was never really a relationship in the first place. It was a transaction. He provided, I complied. That's all it was. That's all it is.

But I have to say something. I have to give them what they want. So, fine. I'll give them the words. I'll explain them. And then we can move on. Because that's what I do. I move on. I don't dwell on the past. I don't wallow in emotions. I just move forward. That's who I am. The engine. If I stop, the whole thing stops. So I don't stop. I just keep going. Always forward. Never back. Never down. Just forward. Always forward.
response_text:
Frankly, this feels like a waste of time. But fine. Reserved, dependable, strict, proud, and—if we're being honest—distant. That about covers it.

Reserved because he wasn't big on emotions. Dependable because he always showed up for his shift. Strict because rules were rules. Proud because, in his own way, he was. And distant because, well, we weren't exactly close. There. Happy? Can we move on now?

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. monologue_text:
Five words. Fine, I can do that. But why? What's the point of this? It's not like I'm going to say "distant" or "cold" or any of those therapy clichés. He wasn't like that. He was... steady. Five words. Okay. "Disciplined." That's one. He was strict, but fair. "Hardworking." Obviously. "Respectful." He demanded respect, and he gave it in return. "Reserved." He wasn't one for emotions. "Proud." He was proud of me, in his own way. That should cover it.

But why does it matter? What does this have to do with anything? It's not like understanding my father is going to help close the deal. But if I don't play along, what does that say about me? That I'm not a team player? That I can't handle a little introspection?

No. I can do this. It's just words. Just a task. I can compartmentalize. But why now? Why this? It's not like I'm going to suddenly have some breakthrough and start crying or something. That's not me. I'm the engine. I keep things moving.

But what if I do this and it opens up something I don't want to deal with? What if I say the wrong thing and it changes how they see me? I don't need that. I need to stay focused. On the deal. On the goal.

But I can't say no. That would be worse. So fine. Five words. Let's get this over with. But I'm not going to overthink it. Just say the words and move on. That's what I do. I move on.
response_text: Disciplined. Hardworking. Respectful. Reserved. Proud. That covers it. My father was a man of routine, of duty. He didn't waste words on sentiment. Those words capture what mattered to him, and to me. Let's move on.

monologue_text: Parents. Again. As if they matter. It's a question, so I should answer it. But God, I hate talking about them.  They're dead now. Both of them. prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Hmm. That's a pretty direct question. I mean, it's not like I don't talk about my mom's mom, but it's not exactly the kind of thing that comes up in casual conversation. They're trying to understand me, I guess. That's... nice? Or maybe they're just being thorough. I don't know.

I could tell them about Grandma. About how Mom came back from the funeral and just sat in the living room for hours, staring at the wall. Or how I had to learn to cook Nigerian food from YouTube because Mom couldn't bring herself to do it. But is that too much? We're just getting to know each other. Maybe they're not ready for all that.

On the other hand, they did ask. And it's not like I'm ashamed of it. It's just... heavy. I don't want to put a damper on things. But if I don't say anything, will they think I'm hiding something? Or worse, that I don't trust them enough to share?

I could make a joke out of it. Something like, "Oh, just the usual childhood trauma, you know?" But that might come off as deflecting. I don't want to seem like I'm making light of it if they're being serious.

But what if they ask more questions? What if they want to know more about Mom? I don't know if I'm ready to talk about that yet. I mean, I'm over it, right? It's been years. But sometimes, when I think about it, I still feel that little kid inside me, the one who didn't understand why Mom was so sad all the time.

Maybe I should just say no. Maybe I didn't lose anyone close when I was a kid. Not in the way they're asking about. I mean, sure, Grandma died, but I was a teenager by then. That's not what they're asking about. That's not a young child.

But then again, maybe it is. Maybe the loss of a grandparent can feel just as big when you're young. Maybe it's not about the age, but the impact. I don't know. I'm overthinking this.

I should just answer the question. Yes, I did. My grandma died when I was a kid. And it was hard. But I'm okay now. I can talk about it if they want. But I don't have to. It's up to me.

I wonder if they've experienced something like that. Maybe that's why they're asking. Maybe they're trying to connect on a deeper level. Or maybe they're just trying to be thorough. I don't know.

I should just answer. Yes, I did. And I'm okay. Let's move on.
response_text: Yeah, my grandma died when I was a teenager. It was rough, especially for my mom. But we got through it. She was a hell of a woman, though—used to send me these outrageous Nigerian outfits that I'd never wear, but my mom would make me try them on just to see her laugh. Miss that lady.

They want to understand me better. Fine. But this isn't how you build trust. Trust is built on results. On performance. On numbers. Not on... whatever this is. It's a distraction. prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text: Oh, that's a deep one. I mean, I was the kid who made the teacher laugh, right? But was that because I was loved, or because I was performing? And what's the difference, really? Mom always said I had a way of lighting up a room, but was that her, or was that me? And why am I thinking about Theo right now? Because he used to say I was too much, that I needed to tone it down. But that's not rejection, that's just... Theo being Theo. Or is it? I mean, he left. And I didn't go. So who's rejecting who there? And why am I getting defensive about a question that's not even about Theo? It's not about Theo. It's about... me. As a kid. I remember the other kids saying I talked funny. But I didn't care. I was too busy making the teacher laugh. So no, I don't think I felt rejected. But maybe I did. Maybe I just didn't let it show. Or maybe I did, and I just don't remember. Because who wants to remember feeling like that? Not me. I'm a comedian, not a therapist. But maybe that's why I'm a comedian. To make sure no one ever feels that way again. Or to make sure I never feel that way again. Either way, it's not about Theo. It's about me. And I'm not sure I want to answer this question. But I will. Because that's what I do. I answer questions. Even the hard ones. Even the ones that make me think about Theo.
response_text: Well, I mean, I was the kid who could get a teacher to cry-laugh, you know? So I guess I never really felt rejected in that way. prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Experiences with parents. Right. obviously affected. Every parent does. It's not… they're not a vacuum, are they? But what's the answer they want to hear. Not that it matters. They'll decide they understand me regardless. Always do. I'm efficient. That's the word they'd use. "Marcus is very efficient." Like it's a compliment. They don't see the failure inherent. Always. How to explain that to someone who… doesn't. Doesn't think that way. They'd think I was just complaining. "Marcus, you're overthinking it." No. You're not thinking at all.

But, no, they're not wrong. Parents do affect you. Father worked himself to death. Literally. Mother… she was there. Except when. She wasn't. Teach fourth grade, come home, cook, clean. No time for. Anything else. Not her fault. Steel town. Penn scholarship. Proud of me. Afraid of me. Why? Because I'm better. That's. That's all. It's not my fault. But they don't. They'd see it as a reason. Not an explanation.

They want a story. I don't have one. Or if I do, it's not the kind they'd. That they'd want. What do they. What do they say? "Marcus, you're not a car. You don't have to keep going like this." But that's the. That's the point. I am. If I stop, everything. The whole thing. The whole. The whole machine. But they. They think. They think it's just about. Just about me.

Why do they. Why do they ask these questions? What's the. What's the answer they want? I don't. I don't know. Do they think. Do they think I'm hiding something? Or. Or do they just. Just want to. To connect. That's the word. Connect. Like we're. Like we're each. Electrodes. In a circuit. Just. Just waiting. For the current. To flow. Between.

And the. And the funniest thing is. The funniest thing. They think. They think. They think it's their job to. To make the connection. Not mine. But what if. What if I don't. What if I don't want. To.
response_text: Frankly, I don’t waste time analyzing it. My parents did what they could. My father worked himself to death, and my mother… she was there when she could be. It shaped me, sure, but the point is to move forward, not to dissect every influence. I am what I am.

monologue_text: Wonder if I can spin this into a bit.
response_text: Oh, where do I even start? My mom was this tiny powerhouse of a woman who ran the school band and the church choir like she was conducting the Berlin Philharmonic. You know, like a good comedian does. Plus, I've got this weird talent for showing up where I'm needed, which I like to think comes from Mom's relentless sense of duty. prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Childhood. What does that even mean? A series of events I outgrew. What's the point of dwelling on it? They want a story, some sentimental bullshit about how I turned out this way. But I didn't turn out this way because of anything that happened to me. I turned out this way because I decided to. Because I saw what was necessary and I made it happen.

They're looking for some vulnerability, some weakness to latch onto. That's what people do when they ask these questions. They want to find the crack in the foundation so they can pry it open. But there is no crack. There's just the engine, running smoothly, efficiently. No distractions, no sentimentality.

What did I learn? That time is a resource, and it's limited. That every moment spent not moving forward is a moment wasted. That people will let you down if you let them. That's not a lesson, that's just a fact. A fact I use to my advantage.

They won't understand that. They want some heartfelt confession, some moment of shared humanity. But humanity is overrated. Efficiency is what matters. Results. The ability to keep moving, to keep pushing forward no matter what.

What do I tell them? Something vague, something that sounds profound but doesn't give anything away. Something that will satisfy their curiosity without revealing anything real. That's the key. Keep them at arm's length, keep them guessing. Never let them see the engine.
response_text:
Frankly, I don't see the point in dwelling on the past. What's done is done. I learned that time is a limited resource, and I choose to spend it moving forward.

The past is just a series of events. What matters is how you use those events to drive yourself forward. That's what I've done. That's what anyone should do.
